# Molten-Salt Reactor Survey

## Operating Principles

Molten-salt reactors dissolve the fissile material directly in a liquid fluoride or chloride salt that also serves as the coolant. Unlike solid-fuel designs, the fuel cannot overheat and fracture because it is already liquid; fission products dissolve into the salt rather than accumulating in a solid matrix that can crack.

## Passive Safety Mechanisms

Most molten-salt designs include a freeze plug — a section of salt kept solid by active cooling. If cooling power is lost, the plug melts and the fuel salt drains by gravity into a subcritical storage tank. Because the drainage requires no operator action and no pump, the design class is described as inherently safe. The storage tank geometry is chosen so that fission stops once the salt enters it.

## Thermal Hydraulics

The low operating pressure of molten-salt reactors simplifies pressure-vessel design and eliminates the risk of a steam explosion. Pumping the salt through the primary loop requires modest energy, and the viscosity of the salt at operating temperature is comparable to water. Load following is straightforward because the salt inventory can be adjusted.

## Development History

Several prototype molten-salt reactors operated in the 1950s and 1960s. Interest revived in the 2000s when the Generation IV International Forum identified the molten-salt reactor as one of six reference designs for next-generation nuclear power. Numerous private ventures have since pursued commercialisation, citing the passive safety characteristics and the potential for thorium use.
